3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image created using Meta AIThe performance difference between open weights and the frontier models has narrowed. Also, there are tremendous cost-savings opportunities moving workloads from frontier models to open weight.
Kimi K3, by Chinese company Moonshot AI, is the clearest proof point yet. Here’s what the actual benchmark data shows:
1). Second only to Anthropic’s Fable 5 on agentic knowledge work
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Worth noting: It averages $10.57 per task and nearly an hour of runtime, making it more expensive and slower than Opus 4.8 on specific workloads, driven by heavy turn usage.
2). #1 Overall on 3D design and data visualization
On Design Arena, which evaluates models on building interactive, browser-based 3D and data-viz experiences from real user prompts, K3 took the top spot. This puts it ahead of Anthropic’s Fable 5.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

3). #3 in the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5
Press enter or click to view image in full size

K3 averages $0.94 per task, roughly half of Opus 4.8’s $1.80, and similar to GPT-5.6 Sol’s $1.04. It also uses 21% fewer output tokens than its predecessor while scoring 13 points higher.
Kimi K3 has temporarily halted new signups due to overwhelming demand (as of July 23rd).
On OpenRouter (10+M user base, it is a model routing service providing access to 400+ LLMs) the top five most-used models by weekly and monthly token volume are now all open-weight:

• Hy3 (free) by Tencent, MiMo-V2.5 by Xiaomi, DeepSeek V4 Flash by DeepSeek, GLM 5.2 by Z-ai, and MiniMax M3 by MiniMax

Open-Weight LLMs are replacing frontier models and this trend will continue. However, the role is shifting. Instead of acting as the default for every task, frontier AI Models will become the elite reserve for complex and edge cases, while open-weight models handle the bulk of the tasks. The future belongs to those who know how to route between both.

Sources: Artificial Analysis, Design Arena, Kimi K3, Openrouter, and Tech Times
